stem: A \( 3 \times 3 \) matrix \( A \) has trace \( [[tr]] \). Two of its three eigenvalues are \( [[l1]] \) and \( [[l2]] \). What is the third eigenvalue?
solution: The trace equals the sum of the eigenvalues, so the third eigenvalue is \( [[tr]] - ([[l1]]) - ([[l2]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the trace itself; you must subtract the two known eigenvalues from it.
This subtracts only one of the two known eigenvalues from the trace.
This adds the two known eigenvalues instead of subtracting them from the trace.
This adds the known eigenvalues to the trace instead of subtracting them.
This is one more than the correct third eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct third eigenvalue.
This is three more than the correct third eigenvalue.

stem: A \( 3 \times 3 \) matrix \( A \) has trace \( [[tr]] \). Two of its three eigenvalues sum to \( [[ssum]] \) and differ by \( [[sdiff]] \). What is the third eigenvalue?
solution: The two unknown eigenvalues' individual values are not needed, only their sum: the trace equals the sum of all three eigenvalues, so the third eigenvalue is \( [[tr]] - [[ssum]] = [[answer]] \) (the given difference \( [[sdiff]] \) is not needed).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly subtracts the difference of the two known eigenvalues instead of their sum.
This adds the sum of the two known eigenvalues to the trace instead of subtracting it.
This is just the sum of the two known eigenvalues, not the trace minus that sum.
This is the trace itself; you must still subtract the known eigenvalues' sum.
This is one more than the correct third eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct third eigenvalue.
This is two more than the correct third eigenvalue.

stem: The characteristic polynomial of a \( 3 \times 3 \) matrix \( A \) is \( \lambda^{3} - ([[tr]])\lambda^{2} + c_1\lambda - c_0 \) for some constants \( c_1, c_0 \). Two of \( A \)'s eigenvalues are \( [[l1]] \) and \( [[l2]] \). What is the third eigenvalue?
solution: The coefficient of \( \lambda^{2} \) in the characteristic polynomial (with a minus sign) is the trace, i.e. the sum of the eigenvalues, so the trace here is \( [[tr]] \). The third eigenvalue is therefore \( [[tr]] - ([[l1]]) - ([[l2]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the negative of the trace (forgetting the polynomial's built-in minus sign) instead of the trace itself.
This subtracts only one of the two known eigenvalues from the trace.
This adds the two known eigenvalues instead of subtracting them from the trace.
This adds the known eigenvalues to the trace instead of subtracting them.
This is one more than the correct third eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct third eigenvalue.
This is four more than the correct third eigenvalue.

stem: A \( 2 \times 2 \) matrix \( A \) has one eigenvalue equal to \( [[l1]] \). The trace of \( [[k]]A \) (that is, \( A \) scaled by \( [[k]] \)) is \( [[m]] \). What is the other eigenvalue of \( A \)?
solution: Since \( \mathrm{trace}([[k]]A) = [[k]]\cdot\mathrm{trace}(A) \), we get \( \mathrm{trace}(A) = [[m]]/[[k]] \). The other eigenvalue is \( \mathrm{trace}(A) - [[l1]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide the given trace of \( [[k]]A \) by \( [[k]] \) before subtracting.
This stops after dividing by \( [[k]] \) without subtracting the known eigenvalue.
This just repeats the known eigenvalue instead of finding the other one.
This multiplies the known eigenvalue by \( [[k]] \) instead of using the trace relationship.
This is one more than the correct other eigenvalue.
This is two less than the correct other eigenvalue.
This is three more than the correct other eigenvalue.
